title: Dental and Facial Injuries: What to Do
summary: Recognize and manage knocked-out teeth, cuts, nosebleeds, and possible facial fractures — and know the minutes that decide whether a tooth can be saved.

sections.heading: Why the mouth and face are high-stakes
body: The face and mouth are exposed in almost every contact sport — a stray elbow, a ball off the face, a fall on the court, a collision at the plate. Facial injuries range from minor (a split lip) to genuinely time-critical (a tooth knocked completely out of its socket).

Most facial injuries look worse than they are because the face bleeds heavily even from small cuts. But a few specific injuries reward fast, correct action in the first minutes — most of all a knocked-out permanent tooth, where what you do in the first 30-60 minutes can be the difference between saving the tooth and losing it for good.

sections.heading: Common injuries you'll see
body: Facial trauma covers a range of injuries with different urgency levels. Knowing which is which helps you respond correctly and quickly.
list: Avulsed tooth — a tooth completely knocked out of its socket. Time-critical; see the next section.

Chipped or fractured tooth — a piece of the tooth breaks off but the tooth is still in the socket. Save any broken fragment if you can find it and get the athlete to a dentist, but this is not an emergency in the same way as an avulsion.

Lacerations (cuts) of the lip, cheek, or tongue — common from teeth cutting into soft tissue on impact. Bleed a lot because the mouth has a rich blood supply, but most are manageable with direct pressure.
Nosebleeds (epistaxis) — very common, usually from a direct blow. Almost always manageable on the sideline.
Suspected jaw (mandible) or cheekbone (zygomatic) fracture — a break in the facial bones. Needs urgent medical evaluation, not just a dentist.

sections.heading: The headline skill: a knocked-out permanent tooth
body: An avulsed (completely knocked out) permanent tooth is a dental emergency. The tooth's root is covered in a thin layer of living cells (the periodontal ligament) that keep it able to reattach — but those cells start dying within the first hour outside the mouth. Re-implantation odds drop fast for every extra minute the tooth is dry or handled wrong.
list: Find the tooth. Pick it up only by the crown (the white chewing surface) — never touch or scrub the root, the narrow part that was in the socket. Touching the root damages the ligament cells the tooth needs to reattach.
If the tooth is dirty, rinse it gently for a few seconds with milk or saline — never scrub it, never use soap, and don't dry it or wrap it in a tissue.

If the athlete is alert and it's a permanent (adult) tooth, try to reinsert it into the socket immediately, pushing it gently back into place, then have the athlete bite down softly on a clean cloth to hold it. This is the single best outcome — a tooth re-implanted within minutes has the best chance of surviving.

If reinsertion isn't possible (athlete is disoriented, the tooth won't seat, you're not confident doing it), store the tooth in cold milk. Milk has the right chemistry and temperature to keep the root cells alive. If milk isn't available, the athlete's own saliva (holding the tooth inside the cheek, not swallowing it) or a commercial tooth-preservation product both work. Never store it in tap water — water's chemistry damages the root cells on contact.
Get the athlete and the tooth to a dentist or emergency room within 30-60 minutes. This is a race against time — call ahead if possible so they're ready.

callout.title: Primary (baby) teeth are different
callout.text: Never try to reinsert a knocked-out primary (baby) tooth. Reinserting it can damage the permanent tooth developing underneath. Still get the athlete to a dentist to check the socket and surrounding teeth, but do not attempt reimplantation.

sections.heading: Chipped teeth and soft-tissue cuts
body: For a chipped or fractured tooth: look for the broken piece — if you find it, store it the same way as an avulsed tooth (milk or saliva) since a dentist may be able to bond it back on. Rinse the athlete's mouth gently with water and get them to a dentist, ideally the same day, especially if the tooth looks sensitive to air or the athlete reports pain — that can mean the fracture reached the nerve.

For cuts to the lip, cheek, or tongue: apply firm, direct pressure with clean gauze for several minutes. Most bleeding slows significantly within 5-10 minutes of steady pressure. A cut through the lip border (the line where the lip meets the skin), a gash longer than about half an inch, or a deep tongue laceration usually needs stitches — refer to a physician or urgent care rather than just letting it close on its own.

sections.heading: Nosebleeds
body: Most sideline nosebleeds come from a direct hit and involve small blood vessels just inside the nostril — they look dramatic but are rarely serious.

Have the athlete sit up and lean slightly forward, not tilt the head back. Tilting back lets blood run down the throat, which can cause choking, gagging, or swallowed blood that upsets the stomach and can be mistaken for a bigger bleed than it is.

Pinch the soft part of the nose (just below the bony bridge) firmly between thumb and finger and hold steady, uninterrupted pressure for a full 10-15 minutes by the clock. Resist the urge to check early — releasing pressure to peek restarts the bleeding. Ice on the bridge of the nose can help too.
If bleeding hasn't slowed after 15-20 minutes of correct pressure, or it's heavy enough that the athlete is swallowing large amounts of blood, refer for medical care.

sections.heading: Red flags: when it's more than a dentist can handle
body: Some facial injuries need urgent medical evaluation (urgent care or the emergency room) rather than a routine dentist visit, because they suggest a broken facial bone or a brain injury alongside the facial trauma.

callout.title: Refer for urgent/ER evaluation
callout.text: Send the athlete for urgent medical care, not just a dentist, if you see: visible facial deformity or asymmetry; the athlete can't close their jaw normally or their teeth don't line up the way they used to (a classic sign of a jaw fracture); significant or rapidly increasing facial swelling; numbness in the face, lip, or chin; double vision or a sunken-looking eye (possible cheekbone/orbital fracture); or any loss of consciousness at the time of injury. A hit hard enough to break facial bones or knock out teeth is also hard enough to cause a concussion — any athlete with facial trauma and a blow to the head needs a full concussion check, not just a mouth exam.

sections.heading: Prevention: mouthguards
body: A properly fitted mouthguard is the single best tool for preventing dental and some facial injuries, cushioning the teeth and jaw from direct impact.

Boil-and-bite mouthguards (softened in hot water, then bitten into shape) are far better than no mouthguard and are the standard for most team sports. Custom-fitted mouthguards, made from a dentist's mold of the athlete's teeth, fit tighter, stay in place better during contact, and are the best option for athletes in high-contact sports or those with braces.

Football, ice hockey, boxing, and lacrosse mandate mouthguards by rule in most leagues. Many other contact and collision sports — wrestling, field hockey, basketball — strongly recommend them even where not required, because a stray elbow or ball is just as likely to cause a dental injury in a 'non-contact' moment of a contact sport.

quiz.question: A player's permanent front tooth is knocked completely out during a game. Which part of the tooth should you hold it by?
choices: The root, so the crown stays clean
The crown (the white chewing surface), never the root
It doesn't matter as long as you're quick
Wrap it in a tissue and don't touch it at all
quiz.explanation: The root is covered in living cells that let the tooth reattach; touching or scrubbing the root damages those cells. Always handle an avulsed tooth by the crown.

quiz.question: If you can't safely reinsert a knocked-out permanent tooth right away, what's the best way to transport it to the dentist?
choices: Wrapped dry in a paper towel
Submerged in tap water
In cold milk (or the athlete's saliva, or a tooth-preservation product)
Packed in ice directly against the tooth
quiz.explanation: Milk has the right chemistry and temperature to keep the root's ligament cells alive. Tap water damages those cells on contact, and letting the tooth dry out kills them quickly.

quiz.question: Within roughly what window should a knocked-out permanent tooth reach a dentist to have the best chance of successful re-implantation?
choices: 30-60 minutes
6-8 hours
By the next day is fine
There's no real time pressure once the tooth is stored properly
quiz.explanation: The periodontal ligament cells on the root start dying within about an hour outside the mouth. Getting the athlete and tooth to a dentist within 30-60 minutes gives the best odds of saving the tooth.

quiz.question: A 12-year-old's baby (primary) tooth gets knocked out during recess sports. What should you do?
choices: Reinsert it immediately, just like a permanent tooth
Store it in milk and try to reinsert it within an hour
Do not reinsert it — store it if you want, but get the child to a dentist to check the area
Nothing needs to be done since baby teeth fall out anyway
quiz.explanation: Reinserting a primary tooth can damage the permanent tooth developing underneath it, so primary teeth are never reimplanted. Still get the athlete checked by a dentist.

quiz.question: Correct sideline management of a nosebleed includes which of the following?
choices: Tilt the head back and pinch the bony bridge of the nose
Sit forward and pinch the soft part of the nose with steady pressure for several minutes
Lie the athlete flat and apply ice to the forehead only
Have the athlete blow their nose repeatedly to clear clots
quiz.explanation: Sitting forward keeps blood from running down the throat, and steady pressure on the soft part of the nose (not the bony bridge) for a full 10-15 minutes controls most nosebleeds. Tilting back and blowing the nose both make things worse.

quiz.question: An athlete takes an elbow to the face. You notice their bite doesn't line up the way it used to and there's visible facial asymmetry. What's the right call?
choices: Ice it and have them try to play through it
Send them to their regular dentist sometime this week
Refer for urgent medical evaluation — this suggests a possible facial fracture
Have them rinse with saltwater and monitor overnight
quiz.explanation: A bite that no longer aligns plus visible deformity are classic signs of a possible jaw fracture, which needs urgent care or the ER — not a routine dentist visit and definitely not continued play.

quiz.question: Why should any athlete with significant facial trauma and a blow to the head also get a concussion check?
choices: Concussion checks are required after any bleeding, regardless of location
A hit hard enough to injure the face or teeth is often hard enough to also affect the brain
It's not actually necessary if the athlete's mouth injury is minor
Concussion and dental injuries always occur together
quiz.explanation: The force needed to fracture facial bones or knock out teeth is often enough to also cause a concussion, so facial trauma with a head impact should prompt a full concussion evaluation, not just a mouth exam.

quiz.question: Why is a custom-fitted mouthguard generally considered better protection than a boil-and-bite mouthguard?
choices: Boil-and-bite mouthguards are not allowed in most sports
Custom mouthguards are cheaper and easier to find
It fits more precisely and stays in place better during contact, especially for athletes with braces
Boil-and-bite mouthguards cannot be used more than once
quiz.explanation: A dentist-made custom mouthguard fits an athlete's teeth more precisely and is less likely to shift or come loose on impact than a boil-and-bite guard. Boil-and-bite is still far better than no mouthguard at all.

flashcards.back: A tooth completely knocked out of its socket — a time-critical dental emergency for permanent teeth.

flashcards.front: How to hold a knocked-out tooth
flashcards.back: By the crown (white chewing surface) only — never touch or scrub the root.

flashcards.front: Best storage for an avulsed tooth if you can't reinsert it
flashcards.back: Cold milk (saliva or a tooth-preservation product also work). Never tap water — it damages the root's living cells.
Get the tooth and athlete to a dentist within 30-60 minutes — root ligament cells start dying within about an hour.
The thin layer of living cells on a tooth's root that lets it reattach to the socket — damaged by touching the root or drying it out.
Never reinsert — it can damage the permanent tooth developing underneath. Still see a dentist.
Sit forward, not tilt the head back — tilting back lets blood run down the throat.
Pinch the soft part of the nose (below the bony bridge) with steady, uninterrupted pressure for 10-15 minutes.
Facial deformity or asymmetry, bite that no longer aligns, significant swelling, facial/lip numbness — needs urgent care, not just a dentist.
Also do a full concussion check — the force that injures the face is often enough to also affect the brain.
Boil-and-bite beats no mouthguard; a dentist-made custom mouthguard fits tighter and protects better, especially with braces.
If found, store it the same way as an avulsed tooth (milk/saliva) — a dentist may be able to bond it back on.
